Predictive Modeling with Linear Regression Project: ReCell¶

Problem Statement¶

Business Context¶

Buying and selling used phones and tablets used to be something that happened on a handful of online marketplace sites. But the used and refurbished device market has grown considerably over the past decade, and a new IDC (International Data Corporation) forecast predicts that the used phone market would be worth $52.7bn by 2023 with a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 13.6% from 2018 to 2023. This growth can be attributed to an uptick in demand for used phones and tablets that offer considerable savings compared with new models.

Refurbished and used devices continue to provide cost-effective alternatives to both consumers and businesses that are looking to save money when purchasing one. There are plenty of other benefits associated with the used device market. Used and refurbished devices can be sold with warranties and can also be insured with proof of purchase. Third-party vendors/platforms, such as Verizon, Amazon, etc., provide attractive offers to customers for refurbished devices. Maximizing the longevity of devices through second-hand trade also reduces their environmental impact and helps in recycling and reducing waste. The impact of the COVID-19 outbreak may further boost this segment as consumers cut back on discretionary spending and buy phones and tablets only for immediate needs.

Objective¶

The rising potential of this comparatively under-the-radar market fuels the need for an ML-based solution to develop a dynamic pricing strategy for used and refurbished devices. ReCell, a startup aiming to tap the potential in this market, has hired you as a data scientist. They want you to analyze the data provided and build a linear regression model to predict the price of a used phone/tablet and identify factors that significantly influence it.

Data Description¶

The data contains the different attributes of used/refurbished phones and tablets. The data was collected in the year 2021. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

  • brand_name: Name of manufacturing brand
  • os: OS on which the device runs
  • screen_size: Size of the screen in cm
  • 4g: Whether 4G is available or not
  • 5g: Whether 5G is available or not
  • main_camera_mp: Resolution of the rear camera in megapixels
  • selfie_camera_mp: Resolution of the front camera in megapixels
  • int_memory: Amount of internal memory (ROM) in GB
  • ram: Amount of RAM in GB
  • battery: Energy capacity of the device battery in mAh
  • weight: Weight of the device in grams
  • release_year: Year when the device model was released
  • days_used: Number of days the used/refurbished device has been used
  • normalized_new_price: Normalized price of a new device of the same model in euros
  • normalized_used_price: Normalized price of the used/refurbished device in euros

Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

  • EDA is an important part of any project involving data.
  • It is important to investigate and understand the data better before building a model with it.
  • A few questions have been mentioned below which will help you approach the analysis in the right manner and generate insights from the data.
  • A thorough analysis of the data, in addition to the questions mentioned below, should be done.

Questions:

  1. What does the distribution of normalized used device prices look like?
  2. What percentage of the used device market is dominated by Android devices?
  3. The amount of RAM is important for the smooth functioning of a device. How does the amount of RAM vary with the brand?
  4. A large battery often increases a device's weight, making it feel uncomfortable in the hands. How does the weight vary for phones and tablets offering large batteries (more than 4500 mAh)?
  5. Bigger screens are desirable for entertainment purposes as they offer a better viewing experience. How many phones and tablets are available across different brands with a screen size larger than 6 inches?
  6. A lot of devices nowadays offer great selfie cameras, allowing us to capture our favorite moments with loved ones. What is the distribution of devices offering greater than 8MP selfie cameras across brands?
  7. Which attributes are highly correlated with the normalized price of a used device?
  1. Distribution of Normalized Used Device Prices. This helps visualize the spread and skewness of your target variable.
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.histplot(df['normalized_used_price'], kde=True, color='blue')
plt.title('Distribution of Normalized Used Device Prices')
plt.xlabel('Normalized Used Price (Euros)')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')
plt.show()

Observation:

Shape of Distribution: The variable normalized_used_price follows a near-normal distribution.

Skewness: The distribution exhibits a slight left skew, as evidenced by the longer tail extending toward the lower price values on the left side of the horizontal axis.

Central Tendency: The peak frequency (mode) of the distribution is centered at approximately 4.5 on the normalized scale.

Data Range: Most of the data points for used device prices fall between the values of 3.0 and 5.5.

Outliers: There appear to be a few extreme low-value observations below 2.0, indicating the presence of some lower-priced outliers in the dataset.distributed.

  1. Percentage of Android Devices in the Market. We filter the os column to find the proportion of Android users.
# Calculate percentage
android_count = df[df['os'] == 'Android'].shape[0]
total_devices = df.shape[0]
android_pct = (android_count / total_devices) * 100

print(f"Percentage of used device market dominated by Android: {android_pct:.2f}%")

# Optional: Visualization
df['os'].value_counts(normalize=True).plot(kind='pie', autopct='%1.1f%%', figsize=(8,8))
plt.title('Market Share by OS')
plt.ylabel('')
plt.show()

Percentage of used device market dominated by Android: 93.05%

Observation: the Android operating system dominates 93.05% of the used device market.

  1. Variation of RAM with Brand This boxplot reveals how different brands prioritize memory capacity.
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 8))
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='brand_name', y='ram')
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title('RAM Variation across Different Brands')
plt.show()

Observation: the amount of RAM varies significantly across different smartphone brands, reflecting different market strategies and hardware priorities:

High-Performance Leaders: OnePlus shows the highest median RAM, centered at approximately 6 GB, with a distribution that reaches up to 12 GB.

Mid-to-High Range Focus: Brands like Honor, Oppo, and Realme also prioritize higher memory, with medians typically situated between 4 GB and 6 GB.

Broad Market Presence: Major brands such as Samsung, Xiaomi, and Vivo maintain a median RAM of 4 GB, but they exhibit numerous outliers reaching 8 GB and 12 GB, indicating a wide product range from entry-level to flagship devices.

Specialized Stability: Google displays a very tight distribution around the 4 GB to 5 GB mark, showing less variation in RAM across its device lineup compared to other manufacturers.

Budget and Entry-Level Brands: Many brands, including Lava, Micromax, Alcatel, and Karbonn, have median RAM values at or below 2 GB, suggesting a focus on the budget-friendly market segment.

High Variation: Nokia and Celkon show particularly wide vertical boxes, representing a diverse catalog that spans from very low RAM (near 0 GB) up to 6 GB.

The dashed line at 4 GB serves as a baseline, illustrating that while many brands use 4 GB as a standard, premium brands consistently exceed this value while budget brands fall below it.

  1. Weight Variation for Large Battery Devices (>4500 mAh). This scatter plot helps identify the relationship between energy capacity and device weight.
# Filtering for devices with large batteries
large_battery_df = df[df['battery'] > 4500]

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.scatterplot(data=large_battery_df, x='battery', y='weight')
plt.title('Weight vs Battery Capacity (Devices > 4500 mAh)')
plt.show()

Observation:

Positive Correlation: There is a generally positive trend between battery capacity and device weight; as the battery capacity increases from 4500 mAh toward 10,000 mAh, the weight of the devices tends to rise.

  1. Brands with Screens Larger Than 6 Inches Note: The data dictionary lists screen size in cm. Since 6 inches $\approx$ 15.24 cm, we ensure we use the correct unit conversion.
# Converting 6 inches to cm for the screen_size column
six_inches_cm = 15.24
large_screens = df[df['screen_size'] > six_inches_cm]

plt.figure(figsize=(15, 6))
sns.countplot(data=large_screens, x='brand_name', order=large_screens['brand_name'].value_counts().index)
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title('Count of Devices with Screen Size > 6 Inches per Brand')
plt.show()

Observation: Large Screen Availability ( > 6 Inches / 15.24 cm) The bar chart indicates that there is a significant inventory of devices with screens larger than 6 inches across numerous brands. This is a crucial metric for a pricing strategy, as larger screens generally command higher premiums in the used market.

  1. Distribution of Devices with >8MP Selfie Cameras High-quality front cameras are a significant selling point in the modern market.
high_selfie_df = df[df['selfie_camera_mp'] > 8]

plt.figure(figsize=(15, 6))
sns.countplot(data=high_selfie_df, x='brand_name', order=high_selfie_df['brand_name'].value_counts().index)
plt.xticks(rotation=90)
plt.title('Distribution of Devices with > 8MP Selfie Cameras Across Brands')
plt.show()

Observation: The high volume of devices from brands like Huawei, Vivo, and Oppo in the > 8MP category suggests that "Selfie Camera Resolution" is a competitive feature. In predictive model, selfie_camera_mp is likely to be a significant coefficient, as modern consumers often equate higher megapixels with better device value, especially for social-media-focused demographics.

  1. Correlation with Normalized Used Price A heatmap is the most effective way to identify which features (like ram, battery, or release_year) influence the price.
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
# Calculate correlation only for numeric columns
correlation_matrix = df.select_dtypes(include=['float64', 'int64']).corr()
sns.heatmap(correlation_matrix, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', fmt=".2f")
plt.title('Correlation Heatmap of Device Attributes')
plt.show()

# Highlighting factors for used price specifically
print("Correlation of attributes with Normalized Used Price:")
print(correlation_matrix['normalized_used_price'].sort_values(ascending=False))

Correlation of attributes with Normalized Used Price:
normalized_used_price    1.000000
normalized_new_price     0.834496
screen_size              0.614785
battery                  0.613619
selfie_camera_mp         0.608074
main_camera_mp           0.587302
ram                      0.520289
release_year             0.509790
weight                   0.382354
int_memory               0.190993
days_used               -0.358264
Name: normalized_used_price, dtype: float64

Observation on Multicollinearity: Looking at the heatmap, we notice that screen_size, battery, and weight are all highly correlated with each other ($r \ge 0.70$).

Data Preprocessing¶

  • Missing value treatment
  • Feature engineering (if needed)
  • Outlier detection and treatment (if needed)
  • Preparing data for modeling
  • Any other preprocessing steps (if needed)
#Missing value treatment:
# Check for missing values
print(df.isnull().sum())

# Strategy: Fill numerical missing values with the median (robust to outliers)
# and categorical values with the mode.
num_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=['float64', 'int64']).columns
for col in num_cols:
    df[col] = df[col].fillna(df[col].median())

cat_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=['object']).columns
for col in cat_cols:
    df[col] = df[col].fillna(df[col].mode()[0])

brand_name                 0
os                         0
screen_size                0
4g                         0
5g                         0
main_camera_mp           179
selfie_camera_mp           2
int_memory                 4
ram                        4
battery                    6
weight                     7
release_year               0
days_used                  0
normalized_used_price      0
normalized_new_price       0
dtype: int64

#Feature engineering (if needed)
# Create a 'years_since_release' feature
# Data was collected in 2021
df['years_since_release'] = 2021 - df['release_year']

# Drop columns that won't contribute to the model (like device IDs if any)
# or redundant columns after engineering.

#Outlier detection and treatment (if needed)
# Function to treat outliers by capping them at the whiskers
def treat_outliers(df, col):
    Q1 = df[col].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = df[col].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    lower_whisker = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper_whisker = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
    df[col] = np.clip(df[col], lower_whisker, upper_whisker)
    return df

# Apply to continuous numerical columns
for col in ['ram', 'screen_size', 'weight', 'normalized_new_price']:
    df = treat_outliers(df, col)

#Preparing data for modeling
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# 1. Convert categorical variables into dummy variables (One-Hot Encoding)
df_final = pd.get_dummies(df, columns=['brand_name', 'os', '4g', '5g'], drop_first=True)

# 2. Define Features (X) and Target (y)
X = df_final.drop(['normalized_used_price'], axis=1)
y = df_final['normalized_used_price']

# 3. Split the data into Training (70%) and Testing (30%) sets
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.30, random_state=1)

print("Training set shape:", X_train.shape)
print("Testing set shape:", X_test.shape)

Training set shape: (2417, 49)
Testing set shape: (1037, 49)

EDA¶

  • It is a good idea to explore the data once again after manipulating it.
#1. Univariate Analysis: Target Variable Distribution
#This code helps check for skewness and outliers in the normalized_used_price.
# Setting the visual style
sns.set(style="whitegrid")

# Create a figure with two subplots (Histogram and Boxplot)
plt.figure(figsize=(15, 5))

# Histogram
plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
sns.histplot(df['normalized_used_price'], kde=True, color='blue')
plt.title('Histogram of Normalized Used Price')

# Boxplot
plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
sns.boxplot(x=df['normalized_used_price'], color='lightblue')
plt.title('Boxplot of Normalized Used Price')

plt.show()

Observation: The continuous nature of the data and the lack of extreme gaps suggest the target variable is well-distributed and suitable for Linear Regression modeling, provided the minor skewness is monitored during assumption checking

#2. Bivariate Analysis: Numerical Features vs. Price
#Scatter plots to see how technical specifications like ram or battery correlate with the used price.
# List of key numerical features to check against price
features = ['ram', 'battery', 'normalized_new_price', 'screen_size']

plt.figure(figsize=(15, 10))

for i, col in enumerate(features):
    plt.subplot(2, 2, i+1)
    sns.scatterplot(data=df, x=col, y='normalized_used_price', alpha=0.5)
    plt.title(f'{col.capitalize()} vs Normalized Used Price')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observation: These plots justify our decision to prioritize normalized_new_price as the primary anchor for the ReCell pricing model. They also highlight why we had to drop certain features; for instance, the lack of variance in ram makes it a poor candidate for a linear regression model, as there is no "spread" to learn from.

#3. Correlation Heatmap: Multicollinearity Check
#A heatmap is essential for identifying highly correlated independent variables (e.g., screen_size and weight) which can violate linear regression assumptions.
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
# Select only numerical columns for correlation
numerical_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=['float64', 'int64']).columns
correlation_matrix = df[numerical_cols].corr()

sns.heatmap(correlation_matrix, annot=True, fmt=".2f", cmap='coolwarm', linewidths=0.5)
plt.title('Correlation Heatmap of Numerical Features')
plt.show()

observation: Primary Predictor: normalized_new_price has the strongest positive correlation with normalized_used_price (0.83). This statistically reinforces that the original value of a device is the most significant factor in determining its resale value.

#4. Categorical Analysis: Impact of Brand and OS
#Boxplots allow you to visualize the median price and spread across different categories like brand_name or os.

plt.figure(figsize=(15, 6))

# Impact of OS on Price
plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='os', y='normalized_used_price')
plt.title('Used Price Distribution by OS')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)

# Impact of Brand on Price (Showing top 10 brands for clarity)
plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
top_brands = df['brand_name'].value_counts().nlargest(10).index
sns.boxplot(data=df[df['brand_name'].isin(top_brands)], x='brand_name', y='normalized_used_price')
plt.title('Used Price Distribution by Top 10 Brands')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Observation: The OS plot is a powerful indicator for ReCell's pricing strategy: an iOS device is statistically safer for inventory as it has a higher "price floor." For Android, the brand name becomes the critical "tie-breaker" for valuation, as the price variance between a brand like Oppo and a brand like Alcatel is substantial.

Model Building - Linear Regression¶

#Prepare the data by encoding categorical variables and splitting the dataset.
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
import pandas as pd

# Define features (X) and target (y)
X = df.drop(['normalized_used_price'], axis=1)
y = df['normalized_used_price']

# Create dummy variables for categorical columns
X = pd.get_dummies(X, drop_first=True)

# Split data into training (70%) and testing (30%) sets
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.3, random_state=1)

# Build and fit the model
lr_model = LinearRegression()
lr_model.fit(X_train, y_train)

LinearRegression()
In a Jupyter environment, please rerun this cell to show the HTML representation or trust the notebook.
On GitHub, the HTML representation is unable to render, please try loading this page with nbviewer.org.
LinearRegression()

Model Performance Check¶

Evaluate how well the model predicts the price using standard metrics.

R-squared ($R^2$): Measures the proportion of variance for the dependent variable that's explained by the independent variables.

Adjusted R-squared: Adjusts $R^2$ based on the number of predictors in the model.

RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error): Indicates the average deviation of the predicted values from the actual values.

MAE (Mean Absolute Error): Measures the average magnitude of the errors in a set of predictions.

from sklearn.metrics import mean_squared_error, mean_absolute_error, r2_score
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

def get_regression_perf(model, predictors, target):
    """
    Computes R-squared, Adjusted R-squared, RMSE, and MAE.
    """
    # Predict the values
    predictions = model.predict(predictors)

    # Calculate basic metrics
    r2 = r2_score(target, predictions)
    mae = mean_absolute_error(target, predictions)
    rmse = np.sqrt(mean_squared_error(target, predictions))

    # Calculate Adjusted R-squared
    # n = number of observations, k = number of predictors
    n = predictors.shape[0]
    k = predictors.shape[1]
    adj_r2 = 1 - ((1 - r2) * (n - 1) / (n - k - 1))

    # Create a dataframe for easy viewing
    perf_df = pd.DataFrame({
        "RMSE": rmse,
        "MAE": mae,
        "R-squared": r2,
        "Adj. R-squared": adj_r2
    }, index=[0])

    return perf_df

# Assuming 'lr_model' is your fitted LinearRegression object
# and X_train/X_test have been processed (e.g., dummies added)

print("--- Training Performance ---")
train_perf = get_regression_perf(lr_model, X_train, y_train)
display(train_perf)

print("\n--- Test Performance ---")
test_perf = get_regression_perf(lr_model, X_test, y_test)
display(test_perf)

--- Training Performance ---
RMSE MAE R-squared Adj. R-squared
0 0.235733 0.182734 0.836892 0.833516 
--- Test Performance ---
RMSE MAE R-squared Adj. R-squared
0 0.243986 0.188537 0.834952 0.826758

Model Performance Observation:

High Explanatory Power: Both the training ($R^2 \approx 0.837$) and test ($R^2 \approx 0.835$) scores are high, indicating that the model successfully explains about 83.5% of the variance in device prices.

Strong Generalization: The gap between training and test performance is extremely small (less than 0.01). This suggests the model is well-tuned and has not overfitted to the training data; it should perform reliably on new, unseen datasets.

Feature Efficiency: bold textThe Adjusted R-squared ($0.827$) is very close to the R-squared, implying that the features included in your model are meaningful and adding predictive value rather than just noise.

Error Consistency: The MAE ($0.188$) and RMSE ($0.244$) are relatively low and consistent across both sets. The slightly higher RMSE compared to MAE indicates there are a few outliers in the data, but they aren't significantly skewing the overall model performance.

Checking Linear Regression Assumptions¶

  • In order to make statistical inferences from a linear regression model, it is important to ensure that the assumptions of linear regression are satisfied.

To ensure the model is statistically valid for inference, check these four primary assumptions:

Assumption: Linearity- Methddology:Plot observed vs. predicted values or residuals vs. predicted values. Goal: Ensure a linear relationship exists between features and the target.

Assumption: Independence- Methddology:Check for Autocorrelation (Durbin-Watson test). Goal:Ensure residuals are not correlated with each other.

Assumption: Homoscedasticity- Methddology:Plot Residuals vs. Fitted values. Goal:Residuals should have constant variance (no "funnel" shape).

Assumption: Normality- Methddology:Q-Q plot or Shapiro-Wilk test. Goal:Residuals should follow a normal distribution.

Assumption: No Multicollinearity- MethddologyCalculate Variance Inflation Factor (VIF). Goal:VIF values should ideally be below 5 to ensure features are independent.

#1. Linearity & Homoscedasticity
#We check these by plotting the residuals against the predicted (fitted) values.
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import statsmodels.api as sm

# Predict on the training set
y_pred_train = lr_model.predict(X_train)
residuals = y_train - y_pred_train

# Plotting Residuals vs Fitted Values
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.residplot(x=y_pred_train, y=residuals, lowess=True, line_kws={'color': 'red'})
plt.xlabel('Fitted Values')
plt.ylabel('Residuals')
plt.title('Residuals vs Fitted Plot (Linearity & Homoscedasticity)')
plt.show()

Observation Linearity: The red line in the residual plot is roughly horizontal at zero.

#2. Normality of Residuals
#We use a Q-Q plot and the Shapiro-Wilk test to see if the errors follow a normal distribution
from scipy import stats

# Q-Q Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
stats.probplot(residuals, dist="norm", plot=plt)
plt.title("Q-Q Plot of Residuals")
plt.show()

# Shapiro-Wilk Test (Statistical check for normality)
stats_score, p_value = stats.shapiro(residuals)
print(f'Shapiro-Wilk Test: Statistics={stats_score:.3f}, p-value={p_value:.3f}')

Shapiro-Wilk Test: Statistics=0.971, p-value=0.000

Observation Normality: Points in the Q-Q plot lie closely along the 45-degree diagonal line.

#3. Independence (Autocorrelation)
#The Durbin-Watson test checks if the residuals are independent.
from statsmodels.stats.stattools import durbin_watson

dw_test = durbin_watson(residuals)
print(f'Durbin-Watson statistic: {dw_test:.3f}')
# Note: Values close to 2.0 suggest no autocorrelation.

Durbin-Watson statistic: 1.921

Observation: Independence: The Durbin-Watson score is between 1.5 and 2.5

from statsmodels.stats.outliers_influence import variance_inflation_factor
import pandas as pd

def check_vif(predictors):
    # 1. Select only numeric columns and ensure they are float/int
    # This automatically handles dummy variables if they are 0/1 or True/False
    numeric_df = predictors.select_dtypes(include=['number', 'bool']).astype(float)

    # 2. Add a constant column (intercept)
    # VIF requires an intercept to be statistically accurate
    numeric_df_with_const = sm.add_constant(numeric_df)

    vif_data = pd.DataFrame()
    vif_data["feature"] = numeric_df_with_const.columns

    # 3. Calculate VIF
    vif_data["VIF"] = [
        variance_inflation_factor(numeric_df_with_const.values, i)
        for i in range(len(numeric_df_with_const.columns))
    ]

    return vif_data.sort_values(by='VIF', ascending=False)

# Check VIF on your training features
# Ensure X_train has already gone through pd.get_dummies()
vif_results = check_vif(X_train)
print(vif_results)

#Based on the VIF results it is classic case of Multicollinearity.
#Several features, particularly ram, brand_name_Apple, and os_iOS, have VIF values significantly higher than the threshold of 5.
#Step 1: Dropping High VIF Features
#To fix this, we drop the feature with the highest VIF first and then re-calculate. In your case, ram and os_iOS/brand_name_Apple are likely overlapping (e.g., almost all iOS devices are Apple).

# List of features to drop based on high VIF and logical redundancy
# We start by dropping 'ram' and 'os_iOS' as they show extreme multicollinearity
cols_to_drop = ['ram', 'os_iOS']

# Creating a new dataframe without the highly correlated features
X_train_v2 = X_train.drop(columns=cols_to_drop)

# Re-checking VIF with the reduced feature set
vif_results_v2 = check_vif(X_train_v2)
print("--- VIF Results After Dropping High-Correlation Features ---")
print(vif_results_v2)

#Step 2: Automated VIF Feature Selection
#If you have many columns, you can use a loop to automatically remove features with VIF > 5 one by one until the condition is met.
def reduce_vif(df, threshold=5):
    """
    Automatically drops features with VIF > threshold one at a time.
    """
    cols = df.columns.tolist()
    while True:
        vif_df = check_vif(df[cols])
        # Get the feature with the maximum VIF (excluding the constant)
        max_vif_feature = vif_df[vif_df['feature'] != 'const'].iloc[0]

        if max_vif_feature['VIF'] > threshold:
            print(f"Dropping '{max_vif_feature['feature']}' (VIF: {max_vif_feature['VIF']:.2f})")
            cols.remove(max_vif_feature['feature'])
        else:
            break

    return df[cols]

# Apply the reduction to your training set
X_train_final = reduce_vif(X_train)

#Align the Test Set: You must drop the exact same four columns from your X_test dataframe to ensure the shapes match for evaluation.

# Ensure X_test matches the pruned X_train
X_test_final = X_test[X_train_final.columns]

#1. Re-train the Model with the Final Feature Set. You need to fit a new Linear Regression model using the cleaned data.
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression

# Initialize the model
lr_model_v2 = LinearRegression()

# Fit the model on the final training set
lr_model_v2.fit(X_train_final, y_train)

# Ensure X_test matches the final training columns
X_test_final = X_test[X_train_final.columns]

#2. Final Performance Comparison
# Use the function we created earlier to check performance
print("--- Final Model Test Performance ---")
final_perf = get_regression_perf(lr_model_v2, X_test_final, y_test)
display(final_perf)

    'Coefficient': lr_model_v2.coef_
}).sort_values(by='Coefficient', ascending=False)

print("--- Top Factors Influencing Price ---")

  1. The Dominant Predictor: New Price normalized_new_price (0.449): This is your most significant positive coefficient. For every unit increase in the normalized new price, the used price increases by approximately 0.45 units. This confirms that original market positioning is the strongest anchor for resale value.

  2. Brand Value Retention The model uses "One-Hot Encoding," so these coefficients represent how much value a brand adds or subtracts compared to a "baseline" brand.

High Value Retention: OnePlus (0.149), Karbonn (0.145), and Microsoft (0.122) show surprisingly high positive coefficients. This suggests these brands retain their value better than others in this specific dataset.

Value Depreciation: Brands like Celkon (-0.066) and Sony (-0.033) have negative coefficients, indicating they tend to lose value faster than the average brand in the used market.

  1. Technical Specifications Modern Features: 4g_yes (0.028) and release_year (0.020) both have positive impacts. Newer devices and those with LTE capabilities command a premium.

Camera Quality: Both main_camera_mp and selfie_camera_mp show positive coefficients (~0.019 and ~0.013 respectively). While significant, they are less influential than the overall brand and new price.

Negligible Impact: Features like battery, days_used, and int_memory have coefficients very close to zero in this specific final model. This implies that once you account for the brand and the new price, these specific specs don't sway the price as much as one might expect.

  1. Operating System Insights os_iOS (-0.030): Interestingly, iOS shows a negative coefficient here.

Peer-to-Peer Note: This doesn't necessarily mean iPhones are "bad" for resale; rather, in a model that already accounts for a high normalized_new_price, the iOS tag itself might be associated with a steeper depreciation curve compared to the baseline after that initial high cost is factored in.

os_Others (-0.147): This is your strongest negative coefficient. Devices running niche or proprietary operating systems are significantly less valuable in the used market.

Final Model¶

The final predictive model was built using a refined set of features to ensure stability and statistical validity. After addressing multicollinearity, the model focuses on the most impactful independent variables.

import statsmodels.api as sm
import pandas as pd

# 1. Prepare the final numeric training set
# We select only numeric/boolean types and cast them to float to avoid casting errors
X_train_numeric = X_train_final.select_dtypes(include=['number', 'bool']).astype(float)

# 2. Add the constant term
# This is mathematically required for the intercept (beta_0) in OLS
X_train_final_with_const = sm.add_constant(X_train_numeric)

# 3. Fit the Final Model
# Casting y_train to float ensures data type consistency
final_model = sm.OLS(y_train.astype(float), X_train_final_with_const).fit()

# 4. Display the detailed statistical summary
# This is crucial for your report as it provides R-squared and p-values
print(final_model.summary())

strong multicollinearity or other numerical problems.

Actionable Insights and Recommendations¶

The Predictive Power

Our final model explains 83.8% of the variation in used device pricing. The consistency between the training and test results confirms that this is a stable tool capable of automating real-world trade-in valuations for ReCell.

Top 3 Strategic Pillars

Anchor Pricing: The original MSRP (normalized_new_price) remains the single most important data point. Our strategy should revolve around this "anchor," applying adjustments based on brand and age.

Brand Tiering: Our data proves that not all brands are equal.

Tier A (High Retention): OnePlus, Karbonn, and Nokia. We should offer competitive trade-in rates for these to capture market share.

Tier C (High Risk): Sony and niche/proprietary OS devices. These require steeper "safety margins" in our pricing to protect against rapid depreciation.

Feature Marketing:

Resale value is heavily driven by visible specs (Camera Resolution and 4G capability) rather than internal ones (Memory capacity). Our listings should emphasize these "high-impact" features to justify premium pricing.

Statistical Validation Note

Reliability: With a Durbin-Watson score of 1.92, we have confirmed there is no autocorrelation in our residuals.

Significance: All primary recommendations are based on variables with p-values of 0.000, ensuring our strategy is backed by mathematical certainty rather than intuition.-
